Introduction
Integrative health care focuses on a holistic, interdisciplinary, pa-
tient-centric, whole health approach to health and wellness. There 
is emphasis on prevention of disease, not just treatment of diag-
nosed conditions. In addition, multiple aspects of the person are 
treated at once, sometimes referred to as the mind, body, and 
spirit. Over the years, the use of integrative approaches has grown 
across the United States. A 2022 national health interview survey 
(Table 19.1) found a significant increase in use of complementary 
and integrative health (CIH) modalities compared with 2002.
89 
In addition, 27% of US military veterans use CIH modalities in 
the treatment of chronic musculoskeletal pain.
116 Integrative 
health care takes into consideration the whole person and all 
things relating to the person’s life. This often includes empower-
ing individuals to actively participate in their own health and 
well-being by providing education and resources, but most im -
portantly it involves active listening to glean an understanding of 
their own goals. Doing so requires care providers to undergo a 
cultural change whereby they no longer consider health care to be 
problem focused but rather ascribe to the philosophy of integra-
tive health care. This reduces the reactive nature of diagnosis and 
treatment in modern medicine. Instead, providers include discus-
sions about goal attainment for prevention of disease. These dis -
cussions and direction help individuals to make long-lasting 
changes that proactively promote health.
Oftentimes, providers do not have the opportunity to spend 
enough time with the patient. It is at this point that an integrative 
health coach (IHC) may be useful. These care team members assist 
individuals with goal setting and progress tracking. IHCs are for-
mally trained care providers who meet individually with patients 
to help promote health and wellness. They often use resources such 
as the Circle of Health ( Fig. 19.1). Exploring all components of 
individuals’ health is key to making lifelong changes. When a care 
provider or care team assists with implementing lifelong changes 
that mirror the individuals’ goals to improve health and wellness, 
then a major accomplishment is achieved.
Whole Medical Systems
Integrative health care incorporates practices and philosophies 
from whole medical systems into conventional or Western medi -
cine. Over the centuries, there have been many different types of 
whole medical systems. Each system incorporates a primary phi -
losophy and includes diagnosis and treatment of conditions. In 
this section, we highlight some major whole medical systems.
Traditional Chinese Medicine
T raditional Chinese medicine (TCM) is probably the best known 
of the whole medical systems. It uses specific diagnostic evalua -
tions such as pulse and tongue assessment. T reatments include 
herbal prescriptions and interventions such as acupuncture, mas-
sage, exercise, dietary modifications, and meditation. This prac -
tice, rooted in Taoist philosophy, evolved over thousands of years 
in China. This philosophy uses the concept of balance between 
complementary forces called yin and yang. An imbalance between 
the two will result in disease. While most research is done on the 
specific modalities, a recent study evaluated TCM rehabilitation, 
which included TCM evaluation with tuina and scalp acupunc-
ture. They found improvement in spasticity and activities of daily 
living.
137
Ayurveda
Ayurveda is a whole medical system from India that aims to pro -
vide guidance regarding food and lifestyle to either maintain well-
ness or improve health. It understands that there are energetic 
forces (tridoshas) that influence nature and human beings and 
that there is a strong connection between the mind and the body. 
• Nutr itional supplements and essential oils can interact with 
medication.
• T he rising cost of health care creates opportunities to utilize an 
integrative approach to improve quality of care and reduce 
cost.
19
Integrative Medicine in Rehabilitation
AJIT B. PAI, LORI V. SHUART, AND DAVID F. DRAKE
• I ntegrative health focuses on a holistic, patient-centered 
 approach to health and wellness and is widely used in 
 behavioral health, pain management, and neurorehabilitation.
• I ndividuals can benefit from singular or multiple mind-body 
practices integrated into traditional health care.
SUMMARY PEARLS

Dietary changes along with herbs are often the prescribed remedy. 
Although research on Ayurveda is sparse, a systematic review 
found Ayurveda management, including Panchakarma therapy 
and Ayurveda medications, with other alternative therapies, such 
as acupuncture, yoga, and rehabilitation, improved muscle 
strength, quality of life, range of motion, and neuronal function. 
It reduced depression, stress, and pain with symptom scores.
63
Homeopathy
Homeopathy, developed in Germany in the late 18th century, fo-
cuses on two theories: “like cures like” (i.e., the disease can be treated 
or cured by a substance that produces similar symptoms in healthy 
people) and “law of minimum dose” (i.e., the effect is greater at the 
lowest dose of the medication). Many homeopathic remedies are so 
diluted that no molecules of the original substance remain.
Naturopathy
Naturopathy, or naturopathic medicine, is a medical system that 
emerged from 19th-century Europe that uses a combination of 
traditional practices and health care approaches popular at the 
time. Naturopathic practitioners see people for various health-  
related concerns, including primary care, overall well-being, and 
treatment of illnesses. T reatment approaches include dietary and 
lifestyle changes, stress reduction, herbs and other dietary supple-
ments, and homeopathy, manual therapies, and exercises. They 
may work with or refer patients to conventional health care pro -
viders. Most research does not consider naturopathy as a separate 
intervention from conventional medical practices; however, in 
2019, a systematic review looking at a broad range of diagnoses 
concluded naturopathic multimodality therapies were shown to 
be effective in the treatment of cardiovascular disease, musculo -
skeletal pain, type 2 diabetes mellitus, polycystic ovary syndrome, 
depression, anxiety, and other chronic complex conditions.

SECTION 2  Treatment Techniques and Special Equipment
Functional Medicine
Functional medicine determines how and why illness occurs and 
restores health by addressing the root causes of disease for each 
individual. It is an individualized, patient-centered, science-based 
approach that empowers patients and encourages a patient- 
practitioner relationship, working together, to address the under-
lying causes of disease and promote optimal wellness. A detailed 
understanding of each patient’s genetic, biochemical, and lifestyle 
helps to direct a personalized treatment plan that leads to im -
proved patient outcomes.
Mind-Body Practices
According to the National Center for Complementary and Inte -
grative Health (NCCIH), mind-body practices are “techniques 
that strengthen the connection between the emotional, mental, 
and physical aspects of ourselves.” Mind-body skills are built on 
the understanding of neuroplasticity. The more you practice, the 
more you build neurologic pathways between the body and mind 
and thus create a stronger connection that can change the struc -
ture and function of the brain.
Practices of the Mind
Meditation and Mindfulness
Meditation implies a variety of practices that focus on mind-body 
integration and are used to calm the mind and enhance overall 
well-being. Meditation is a frequently used modality to induce 
relaxation and reduce stress. The primary goal of meditation is to 
focus attention to control thoughts and reduce stress. An added 
benefit is that no special equipment or space is necessary; this 
makes it accessible to anyone. Meditation may involve maintain -
ing mental focus on a particular sensation, such as a sound, a vi -
sual image, breath, or a mantra, which is a repeated word or 
phrase. Other forms of meditation may include the practice of 
mindfulness, which involves maintaining attention or awareness 
on the present moment without making judgments. The most 
common form of meditation is prayer.
In physical medicine and rehabilitation practice, mindfulness 
meditation is often used with individuals who suffer pain or expe-
rienced life-altering traumatic injuries. In a seminal article in 
1982, Jon Kabat-Zinn demonstrated that mindfulness signifi -
cantly improved pain rating in a majority of participants along 
with mood and other psychiatric symptoms. 60 More recently, 
mindfulness meditation was associated with pain reduction in 
patients with chronic low back pain 15,33,57 and fibromyalgia 114 
along with significant improvement in depression, physical 
health–related quality of life, and mental health–related quality of 
life.49 The effects of mindfulness can be conducted in-person or 
via telemedicine with similarly effective results. 32 In addition, for 
individuals with traumatic brain injury (TBI), mindfulness medi-
tation was shown to improve quality of life with sustained im -
provement beyond 1 year.6
Guided Imagery
Guided imagery, or visualization, includes picturing objects, scenes, 
or events that are associated with relaxation, calmness, or movement 
and attempting to produce similar feelings in the body. The 
 American Holistic Nurses Association defines guided imagery as a 
modality that “assists clients in connecting with their inner knowl-
edge at the thinking, feeling, and sensing levels, promoting their 
innate healing abilities. T ogether, guide and client cocreate an effec-
tive way to work with: pain, symptom grief, and stress management; 
conflict resolution; self-empowerment issues; and/or preparing for 
medical-surgical procedures.”44 According to the Academy for 
Guided Imagery, there are three categories of therapeutic application 
for guided imagery: (1) stress reduction and relaxation, (2) active 
visualization or directed imagery, and (3) receptive imagery.
In the rehabilitation world, motor imagery, a subset of active 
visualization or directive imagery, is used to prepare individuals 
for functional movement. Guided imagery reduces overall pain, 
phantom limb pain in amputees, 75 and pain in advanced cancer 
patients. In addition, there is evidence of reduction in anxiety, 66 
length of stay in postoperative orthopedic patients, 20 and im -
provement in frequency, severity, quality of life, and disability 
caused by chronic tension-type headache.80
Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction
Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) is intensive mindful-
ness training, including meditation, yoga, body awareness, 
 behavioral awareness, and emotional awareness. It explores an 
individual’s present experience in relation to current thoughts, 
physical and emotional sensations, and memories to promote 
understanding, acceptance, and reduction of suffering. In adults 
with chronic low back pain, treatment with MBSR compared 
with the control group demonstrated greater improvement in 
back pain and functional limitations at both 26 and 52 weeks. 23 
However, in individuals with Parkinson disease, there is inconclu-
sive evidence for the use of MBSR.82 Veterans with posttraumatic 
stress disorder (PTSD) enrolled in the MBSR program showed a 
reduction in PTSD symptoms and specifically showed increases in 
“acting with awareness” and “nonreactivity.” They also noted in-
creases in mindfulness were strongly related to decreases in hyper-
arousal and emotional numbing.112
Hypnosis
Clinical hypnosis is a heightened state of concentration and fo -
cused attention. Guided only by a trained professional, hypnosis 
enables the recipient to be more open to suggestions to make 
changes in perceptions, emotions, sensations, or behaviors to 
lead toward better health. Recently, more focus has been placed 
upon hypnosis for the use of pain control. In 2018, hypnosis was 
found to provide a statistically significant improvement in pain 
and anxiety intensity for individuals undergoing wound care to 
treat burns. 98 At this time, a wide area of research in hypnosis 
involves palliative care. However, there is no conclusive evidence 
that hypnosis is a primary treatment modality for any medical 
condition.
Prayer
Prayer is the best known and most widely practiced example of 
meditation. It is used with the intention of health promotion and 
alleviation of disease. T raditional prayer allows for a deep state of 
meditation. Intercessory prayer is praying for others. Currently, 
there are inconclusive results as to the effect of prayer on illness. 
One meta-analysis of studies examining the effect of private and 
communal prayer participation suggests that prayer might reduce 
pain intensity.56
Creative Arts Therapies
Creative arts therapies (CATs) include art therapy, music therapy, 
dance/movement therapy, and many other forms of expression. 
These modalities are used to help individuals with illness or injury

to use expression and communication to improve their functioning. 
In persons with stroke, CAT in addition to physical therapy sig -
nificantly decreased depression and improved physical function and 
quality of life compared with physical therapy alone.65 In addition, 
music therapy may improve gait, upper extremity function, com-
munication, and quality of life. 79 For individuals on mechanical 
ventilators, music therapy may have a beneficial effect on anxiety, 
decrease consumption of sedative and analgesic medication, and 
reduce respiratory rate and blood pressure.14
The previously discussed examples are just a sampling of the 
research that exists for practices of the mind. Overall, meditation 
and CATs can be used in many clinical areas to assist with adjust-
ment, pain symptoms, frustration, and more. The incorporation 
of this practice into rehabilitation programs can potentially assist 
individuals with their recovery. However, more research is needed 
to truly demonstrate the effectiveness of these modalities in a va -
riety of conditions that cause functional limitation.
Movement Therapies
Exercise and Mindful Movement
Exercise has been repeatedly shown to reduce the risk for develop-
ment and progression of diseases, illnesses, and mortality. Physical 
activity is applied in recovery of illnesses and deconditioning; in 
addition, it is recognized to provide medicinal properties in the 
preventative and wellness world. In 2007, the American College 
of Sports Medicine collaborated with the American Medical 
 Association and the Office of the Surgeon General to implement 
the Exercise Is Medicine (EIM) campaign. 118 The EIM initiative 
was implemented to encourage practitioners to promote exercise 
or physical activity for treatment of disease and preventative 
health measures.
Although many types of aerobic and anaerobic exercise pro -
mote physical and mental well-being, there are specific types  
of physical activity that incorporate a mind-body approach. 
These practices are often referred to as mindful movement. 
Mindful movement is any application of combined movement of 
 physical-based activity, mindfulness practice, breathing, and 
spiritual and/or energy-generating practice. Mindful movement 
aids in symptomatic management of depression, schizophrenia, 
and other mental health and psychiatric disorders. 72,139 There is 
also a strong relationship between biopsychosocial improvements 
and mindful movement to include enhanced emotional well-  
being and improved social relationships. 11 Although there are 
many types of mindful movement, only a select few are sup -
ported by evidence-based research to justify their use in medi -
cine. The hormone irisin is released from muscle tissue during 
exercise; recent studies show this hormone is also released with 
forms of tai chi, and it can have a significant effect on cognitive 
outcomes with aging adults. 42
In response to the need for nonpharmacologic strategies to 
address chronic low back pain and other chronic pain condi -
tions, a mixed method feasibility trial was conducted integrating 
group therapy, mindfulness, and expressive dance and move -
ment.109 In this study, maladaptive pain-related beliefs and cop -
ing strategies were challenged, and the results demonstrate a 
strong indication for chronic pain management and increased 
motivation for physical activity. Although more research is war -
ranted, the results for mindful movement to address chronic 
pain are very promising.
In a 2024 systematic literature review conducted over a  
10-year period, a combination of exercise, cognitive behavioral 
therapy, and mindfulness-based programs showed a significant 
reduction in pain and fatigue during cancer treatments.13 Specifi-
cally, tai chi and qigong proved beneficial during active oncology 
treatment, whereas yoga had more benefit managing symptoms 
after treatments.
Tai Chi
Tai chi (pronounced “tie-chee”) is a form of martial arts derived 
from ancient traditional Chinese practice. 47 Tai chi involves a se-
ries of movements performed in a slow manner accompanied by 
deep or patterned breathing. Tai chi fosters qi (pronounced 
“chee”), or life energy, within the body with efforts to promote 
harmony of mind and body. As with many martial arts, there are 
several forms of tai chi practiced. The most common forms of tai 
chi include chen, yang, wu, and sun. All styles share the same es -
sential principles but have their own unique characteristics. The 
basic principle of all forms or styles is to integrate the controlled 
breathing with movement, generate energy, and harvest strength 
and balance for both the mind and body.
There have been several evidence-based health benefits researched 
to support the use of tai chi in practice.53 The most prominent ben-
efits for the application of tai chi are for (1) fall prevention, 106,126  
(2) osteoarthritis,130 (3) Parkinson disease, (4) chronic obstructive 
pulmonary disease (COPD), 21 and (5) cognitive function. 136 
 Specifically, the use of tai chi with a combination of various interven-
tions for individuals with Parkinson disease has been shown to 
 improve motor function, depression, quality of life,111 balance and 
well-being,31 and functional fitness.24 Ai chi, a tai chi application in 
an aquatic setting, may help to improve functional mobility, quality 
of life, balance, and motor ability more efficiently than similar land 
applications for those with Parkinson disease.67 There is also good 
evidence supporting health benefits to those with depression, 124 
cardiovascular disease,68 cancer,136 fibromyalgia,107 PTSD and other 
similar anxieties,62 hypertension,94 and osteoporosis.25 Recent re -
search supports the use of tai chi in reducing dyspnea in patients 
with lung cancer undergoing rehabilitation.113
Qigong
Qigong (pronounced “chee-gung”) is another traditional Chinese 
practice of movement encompassing affirmations, breath work, 
meditation, and guided imagery or visualization. Qi means “air or 
breath,” and gong means “effort or work.” In the practice of qi -
gong, the breath is the vital life force that controls everything in 
the universe. This belief correlates with the concept of bioelectric-
ity through the body. Qigong, when put into practice, requires 
time, patience, and repetition. It is believed that individuals will 
develop the ability to control the qi and prevent disease, promote 
self-healing, and increase longevity.
Qigong is proven to be effective for improving exercise capac -
ity for individuals who are deconditioned, decreasing symptoms 
of depression, 22 reducing inflammation, 97 stabilizing blood 
sugar,85 reducing musculoskeletal pain, improving quality of life, 
and improving quality of sleep. 138 Paired with other traditional 
therapies, qigong may decrease recovery times 77,84 and increase 
pulmonary function for persons with COPD. 73
A recent systematic metanalysis identified a variety of exercise- 
and mindfulness-based interventions for impact on cognition and 
quality of life in breast cancer survivors during and after cancer 
treatment.134 This analysis concluded that physical activity and 
qigong improved executive functions over other interventions. 
Mindfulness-based movement practices are proving to be an effec-
tive intervention in cancer rehabilitation programs.

SECTION 2  Treatment Techniques and Special Equipment
Yoga
Yoga is a mindful movement practice that encompasses stretching, 
strengthening poses, meditation, and breathing exercises. Yoga 
uses body, breath, and the senses to reconnect the participant with 
the universe and move emotions and thoughts into stillness. The 
most common forms of yoga include hatha, vinyasa, ashtanga, 
bikram, iyengar, kundalini, yin, viniyoga, anusara, and sivananda. 
Hatha yoga is the most beginner-friendly yoga and is often inte -
grated into rehabilitative practice. Yoga decreases chronic pain, 
stabilizes blood pressure, and reduces stress. Supportive data also 
suggest that yoga improves balance, breathing, spine health, men-
tal health, blood flow, sleep, strength, and flexibility. Currently, 
there is some evidence that the practice of yoga results in improve-
ments in low back pain at 3 and 6 months as compared with 
nonexercise controls.125 However, in persons with stroke, there is 
no conclusive evidence that yoga is effective or safe. 70
In addition, yoga provides positive effects on the cardiac auto-
nomic nervous system while reducing inflammation, decreasing 
stress, and reducing the risk and progression of cardiovascular 
disease.43 There is relatively strong evidence that supports the 
practice of yoga for low back pain and other chronic pain syn -
dromes as a long-term intervention and effect on disability.28 Yoga 
is associated with improved regulation of the sympathetic nervous 
system and the hypothalamic-pituitary system in several popula -
tions and therefore associated with symptoms of stress reduc -
tion.96 Yoga increases range of motion and mobility of the spine; 
consequently, individuals report improved quality of life.41
Pilates
Pilates is a form of mindful movement that was developed by 
Joseph Pilates in the 1920s. He developed the method, once 
called contrology (the science of control), incorporating compo -
nents of gymnastics, boxing, and wrestling. The origin of Pilates 
was for the purpose of rehabilitating soldiers returning home from 
war. Pilates practice emphasizes the development of core strength 
and spinal alignment to improve overall fitness through the 
 coordination of body, mind, and spirit. Pilates can be performed 
independently or in a group, on a mat, or using specialized equip-
ment. Some specialized equipment used in Pilates include the 
Reformer, the Cadillac, and the Wunda chair, using a system of 
pulleys and straps to provide support and resistance.
Regular practice of Pilates can improve posture, muscle tone, 
balance, and joint mobility and relieve stress and tension. For elite 
athletes, including dancers, Pilates can complement training by 
developing whole body strength and flexibility and can help to 
reduce the risk of injury. Pilates is used as an effective rehabilita -
tion tool, supplementing traditional applications, primarily in 
reducing pain and disability.18
Pilates has been proven to be effective in treatment for low 
back pain by improving the lumbopelvic rhythm 81 and strength-
ening the spinal muscles to improve neuromuscular efficiency 95 
and core muscles, including the lower pelvic floor. 38 Pilates is 
useful with patients with chronic neck or back pain as an effective 
tool in the management of pain, disability, and kinesiophobia. 30 
It improves overall function and quality of life and leads to reduc-
tion in the use analgesic pain medication. 34 In rehabilitation, 
 Pilates is more effective than closed kinematic chain exercises for 
reducing pain and improving functional performance with sub -
jects with knee osteoarthritis.83,110
In the older adult population, Pilates is used to improve lung 
function and physical conditioning. 2 Overall, improvement in 
exercise tolerance is seen with clients with chronic diseases using 
regular Pilates.86 Pilates may be beneficial to use as an alternative 
to standard physical activity applications in reducing depression 
and anxiety.123 Pilates, like other forms of exercise, reduces the fall 
risk of older adults by improving static and dynamic balance, 
range of motion, and functional mobility.103
Neurologic Pilates was developed as a modified version of the 
Pilates method to be used for neurologic rehabilitation. Recent 
findings indicate that, over a 3-month period, the integration of 
neurologic Pilates significantly improved static balance, righthand 
manual dexterity, and daily functioning of stroke survivors when 
combined with conventional therapies; however, scientific evi -
dence remains limited.39
Feldenkrais Method
The Feldenkrais method or therapy, developed by Moshe Felden-
krais, is the application of a gentle mindful movement based on 
principles of physics, biomechanics, psychology, motor develop -
ment, martial arts, and the reconnection of self-education to how 
the body moves and develops. Feldenkrais stated, “We move 
 according to our perceived self-image.” By expanding clients’ per-
ception and increasing awareness, the theory is that participants 
will become more aware of their habits and tensions and develop 
new ways of moving. Range of motion, flexibility, and coordina -
tion become easier, and movement thus becomes more efficient.
There are two general types of Feldenkrais practices: (1) aware-
ness through movement and (2) functional integration sessions. 
Awareness through movement uses slow, mindful movements to 
improve strength, flexibility, and integration of the body and 
mind. Somatic education is integrated into every session to relate 
movement to functional daily activities. Functional integration 
sessions use gentle touch to guide the participant through move -
ment patterns enhancing awareness and improving ease and ef -
fectiveness of movement. The principles of body awareness and 
self-discovery are practiced and integrated into Feldenkrais 
 sessions.
Recently, Feldenkrais has been shown to (1) improve efficiency 
of physical movements and self-efficacy48; (2) produce changes in 
muscle tone leading to a more relaxed spine and reduction of 
chronic pain16; (3) stabilize balance, 119 improve quality of life, 117 
and promote mobility and confidence93; and (4) offset age-related 
declines and enhance cognitive function.121 In addition, individu-
als with chronic pain benefit from Feldenkrais due to changes in 
muscle tone, improvements in mobility and balance, and im -
proved confidence in movement through awareness. 91 With 
Feldenkrais, older adults experience improvements in balance, 
mobility, gait, and fall reduction. 122 A systematic review meta -
nalysis of the Feldenkrais method indicated that it is an effective 
treatment of some clinical conditions and pathologies by improv-
ing balance, mobility, and quality of life and showing similar ef -
fects as conventional interventions.7
Biomechanical Therapies
Acupuncture
Acupuncture is a form of energy medicine and is one of the more 
common and more researched of the integrative health modali -
ties. It shares with the others a similar treatment setting and a 
conceptual framework similar to tai chi or qigong, with a life 
energy called qi that is thought to circulate through all parts of the 
body in energy channels called meridians. These meridians con -
nect the exterior to the interior and the organs to each other and 
the exterior. The classical Chinese explanation is that channels of

energy run in regular patterns through the body and over its sur -
face. These energy channels (meridians) are like rivers flowing 
through the body to irrigate and nourish the tissues. An obstruc -
tion in the movement of these energy rivers is like a dam that 
backs up in others. Pain and illness are thought to occur when the 
flow of qi becomes blocked or unbalanced. Acupuncture is a treat-
ment used to reestablish the flow of qi through the placement of 
needles at points along the meridians, thus allowing the body to 
return to homeostasis and easing the ailment for which it was 
prescribed. The meridians can be influenced by needling the acu-
puncture points. The acupuncture needles unblock the obstruc -
tions at the dams and reestablish the regular flow through the 
meridians. Acupuncture treatments can therefore help the body’s 
internal organs to correct imbalances in digestion, absorption, 
and energy production activities and in the circulation of energy 
through the meridians.
The modern scientific explanation is that needling the acu -
puncture points stimulates the nervous system to release chemi -
cals in the muscles, spinal cord, and brain. These chemicals will 
either change the experience of pain or will trigger the release of 
other chemicals and hormones, which influence the body’s own 
internal regulating system. An action potential is also triggered by 
the stimulation of acupuncture needle insertion, which in turn 
affects the activities in the brain’s neuronal network. 131 The 
physiologic reactions of acupuncture effects are beginning to 
emerge. Stimulation of acupuncture points has been shown to 
create signal changes in the amygdala, anterior hippocampus, and 
subgenual cingulate cortex on functional magnetic resonance 
imaging (fMRI).76 Other studies confirm these findings, defining 
the role of the amygdala in affect, fear, and defensive behavior, as 
well as the processing of pain 8,12,55 and motivation. 133 The hip -
pocampus is thought to link affective states with memory process-
ing. The signal decrease within the amygdala and anterior 
 hippocampus is consistent with past acupuncture fMRI studies at 
acupoints LI-4 and GB-34, as well as ST-36. 51,52,128,129,135 Ahsin 
et al. linked electroacupuncture to functional improvement by the 
measured rise in endorphins and fall in cortisol levels in individu-
als given electroacupuncture.1
The improved energy and biochemical balance produced by 
acupuncture results in stimulating the body’s natural healing 
abilities and in promoting physical and emotional well-being. The 
anatomy of acupuncture points is not clear; however, a theory 
proposed by Langevin and Yandow69 suggests that the meridians 
are located within tissues planes. Acupuncture points occur at a 
convergence of these planes, and meridian qi is the biochemical 
and bioelectrical signaling in the connective tissue. A blockage of 
qi may lead to an altered connective tissue matrix composition, 
leading to altered signal transduction and therefore pain or other 
symptoms. They reported that ultrasound imaging reveals visible 
connective tissue forming an intramuscular cleavage plane at acu-
puncture points but not at control points. 69 Another reported a 
distinctive feature of acupuncture points is the high density of 
nerve endings. 46,71 Dorsher did a study based on T ravell’s and 
 Simons’s texts and found that 93.3% of the common myofascial 
trigger points (238 of 255) correspond to the classical acupunc -
ture points.35 The fascial anatomy has a striking anatomic correla-
tion to acupuncture points and meridians. Finando and Finando 
suggested that all fundamental characteristics of acupuncture 
treatment are consistent with stimulation of the fascia. 37
In 2022, an evidence map reviewing acupuncture was pub -
lished and identified 1207 potentially relevant citations and in -
cluded 64 publications, categorized into more than 40 conditions. 
Their general observations include (1) there are more mapped 
conclusions for painful conditions than for all other conditions 
combined, (2) there are only a small number of reviews with at 
least one conclusion rated as high certainty of evidence (CoE),  
(3) the conclusions about the effectiveness of acupuncture that 
were judged to have at least moderate CoE mostly concerned 
comparisons of acupuncture to sham or control acupuncture for 
painful conditions or pain outcomes, and (4) there is no evidence 
that acupuncture is less safe than usual care for these  conditions.108 
Acupuncture is an ancient medical modality, with physiologic 
changes that appear to suggest neurologic and neurochemical ef -
fects that can lead to clinical improvement. Many people may 
benefit from acupuncture, although it is not a panacea.
Osteopathy
Since its founding in the 19th century by Andrew Taylor Still, 
osteopathic medicine, which includes manual manipulation of 
the spine, has been at the forefront of integrative medicine. Still 
stressed the importance of the connection among mental, physi -
cal, emotional, and spiritual health and taught that each plays an 
important role in patients’ overall health. The evidence for man-
ual therapy, as many refer to it, is not overwhelming. However, 
there does appear to be a role for osteopathic medicine in the 
management of pain, particularly if treated early. A 2018 system-
atic review and metanalysis looking at manipulation and mobili -
zation for chronic low back pain, sponsored by the NCCIH, 
 determined there is moderate-quality evidence that manipulation 
and mobilization are likely to reduce pain and improve function 
for patients with chronic low back pain; manipulation appeared 
to produce a larger effect than mobilization.26
Chiropractic
Chiropractic considers the structure of the body and the relation-
ship to function. By using manipulation of the spine or extremi -
ties, alignment is corrected so that pain and function can  improve. 
Oftentimes, chiropractors use nonmanipulation modalities as 
part of their practice. These include heat and ice, electrical stimu-
lation, nutritional counseling, relaxation techniques, and exercise. 
Chiropractic is often used for spinal pain, headaches, joint pain, 
and soft tissue injuries.
Massage
Massage is a general term for pressing, rubbing, and manipulating 
the skin, muscles, tendons, and ligaments to aid in relaxation and 
recovery from injury. Massage therapists typically use their hands 
and fingers but may also use their forearms, elbows, and even feet. 
Swedish massage is primarily used for relaxation and is most com-
mon in spa settings. Thai massage uses assisted stretching. Shiatsu 
is a Japanese technique in which finger pressure, often along me -
ridians that may correspond with acupuncture points, is used. 
Deep tissue massage is used to target trigger points and release 
chronic muscle tension. Sports massage can be used before events 
as part of a warmup, during the event in cases of cramping, and 
after the event as part of the cooldown. Heat can be added to any 
of the above mentioned treatments via warmed stones, topical 
ointments, creams, or other emollients to aid in relaxation.
A systematic review in 2021 that included 18 randomized 
controlled trials (RCTs) comparing Chinese massage (tuina)  
plus conventional physiotherapy with conventional physiother -
apy was completed. They reported improvements in upper  
limb motor function using the Fugl-Meyer Assessment and up -
per and lower limb spasticity using the modified Ashworth scale.

They concluded that therapeutic massage, especially tuina, in 
addition to conventional therapy is effective for improving motor 
function and for reducing spasticity in stroke survivors. 19 In 
2019, an evidence map, which identified 32 high-quality system-
atic reviews of massage to treat pain, concluded that there was 
low strength of evidence of potential benefits of massage for 
labor, shoulder, neck, low back, cancer, arthritis, postoperative, 
delayed-onset muscle soreness, and musculoskeletal pain. They 
reported that the low strength of evidence could be attributed to 
the fact that few primary studies of large samples with rigorous 
methods had been conducted, which leaves evidence gaps about 
specific massage types for specific pain. In addition, they men -
tioned that primary studies often do not provide adequate details 
of massage therapy, limiting the extent to which reviews are able 
to draw conclusions about characteristics such as provider type. 87 
A systematic review of massage in the general pain population 
looked at 60 high-quality and 7 low-quality studies and found 
that massage therapy effectively treats pain compared with sham, 
no treatment, and active controls. Massage therapy was also ben -
eficial for treating anxiety and found to improve health-related 
quality of life. 29
Rolfing
Rolfing, also called Rolf therapy or structural integration, is a 
holistic system of bodywork that uses deep manipulation of the 
body’s soft tissue to realign and balance the body’s myofascial 
structure. There is a paucity of research; however, one study 
showed improved gait in children with spastic cerebral palsy. 78 
Another showed improvement in body image and low back pain 
following three structural integration sessions.5
Biofeedback
Biofeedback is a technique of making unconscious or involuntary 
bodily processes (such as heartbeats or brain waves) perceptible to 
the senses with the hope of manipulating them by conscious men-
tal control. A 2019 evidence map that included 16 good-quality 
systematic reviews examined biofeedback alone or as an adjunc -
tive intervention. They found clear, consistent evidence suggest -
ing that biofeedback is effective for reducing headache pain and 
improving urinary incontinence after prostatectomy. It may pro -
vide benefit for fecal incontinence, balance, and stroke recovery.64
Regenerative Medicine
Regenerative medicine is a branch of medicine concerned with 
developing therapies that regenerate or replace injured, diseased, 
or defective cells, tissues, or organs to restore or establish function 
and structure. Regenerative medicine treatments include stem cell 
therapy to stimulate tissue repair and regeneration at the site of 
damage or the transplantation of tissues or organs made or grown 
outside the body. A meta-analysis of 78 RCTs reported that a re -
duction in pain was associated with platelet-rich plasma (PRP) at 
3 months and sustained until 1 year. More specifically, they men-
tion low- to moderate-quality evidence supporting a reduction in 
pain for lateral epicondylitis and knee osteoarthritis at 1 year, 
noting that PRP characteristics did not influence results. They 
concluded that PRP leads to a reduction in pain; however, evi -
dence for clinically significant efficacy is limited. The available 
evidence currently supports the use of PRP in the management of 
lateral epicondylitis as well as knee osteoarthritis.58 A 2020 meta-
analysis of the effect of PRP on knee lesions selected only 
83   clinical trials using the revised Cochrane risk-of-bias tool to 
evaluate risk. They reported significant differences in favor of PRP 
when compared to the control groups both on the recovery of 
knee disorders and during knee surgery. 120 More recently, there 
has been interest in mesenchymal stem cells as a form of regen -
erative medicine. While this is a growing area of research, there is 
no consistent strong research supporting its use.
Natural Products
Nutrition
Appropriate nutrition is a large part of CIH. There is a concern 
that various foods cause inflammation that can lead to disease 
states. One thing to consider is whether an antiinflammatory diet 
is proper for the individual patient. Eicosanoids are breakdown 
products of fatty acids. One product of the breakdown of 
omega-3 fatty acids is antiinflammatory prostaglandin; however, 
omega-6 fatty acids result in breakdown products that include 
proinflammatory prostaglandins. Omega-3 fatty acids can be 
found in coldwater fish, such as salmon, sardines, herring, and 
mackerel; ground flax seeds or flax seed oil; leafy green vegetables; 
and walnuts. Omega-6 fatty acids are found in red meats, dairy 
products, partially hydrogenated oils, and foods with long shelf 
lives.
Another area of concern is excess free radical production, 
which may lead to tissue damage and inflammation. It is thought 
that food highly rich in antioxidants may help to protect against 
free radical–induced damage.
Dietary Supplements
The dietary supplement (Table 19.2) industry is continually grow-
ing. Dietary supplement use increased between 2007 and 2018 
due to an increase in single-nutrient products, despite a relative 
decrease in multivitamin use. 27 In rehabilitation literature, there 
are multiple studies reviewing the use of dietary supplements in 
various populations. In multiple sclerosis, for example, RCTs 
found the use of polyunsaturated fatty acids (omega-6 or -3) had 
no statistically significant effects on clinical outcomes.3
Persons with spinal cord injury (SCI) use dietary supplementa-
tion at a greater rate than the general population. 92 For years in 
the SCI population, individuals tried cranberry supplementation 
as a preventive mechanism for urinary tract infection (UTI). A 
2017 review of the effectiveness of dietary supplements in the SCI 
population demonstrates there is high-quality, conclusive evi -
dence against the use of cranberry supplementation for preven -
tion of UTIs.90 In addition, this study reported moderate-quality 
evidence for the beneficial effect of vitamin D (increased bone 
mineral density in nonsmokers), alpha-lipoic acid (fasting blood 
sugar, waist circumference, and blood pressure), and omega-3 
supplementation.
In persons with brain injury, dietary supplements are used in -
creasingly. Omega-3 acids are thought to prevent axonal loss and 
are strong antiinflammatory agents. 45 Due to these two mecha -
nisms, there is hope that omega-3 acids can be used prophylacti -
cally in athletes and the military; however, conclusive evidence 
does yet not exist. Another supplement to be considered in TBI is 
zinc. This essential trace element is linked to various neurologic 
disorders and may have promise in the treatment of TBI. One 
study demonstrated improvements in Glasgow Coma Scale scores 
as compared with controls.132

Calcium and strontium contain nearly the same size atomic 
nucleus. As a result, strontium is readily taken up by bones and 
tooth enamel. Strontium ranelate is approved as a prescription 
drug for the treatment of osteoporosis in Europe and other coun-
tries but not in the United States; it is thought to be effective in 
both sexes.
61,100,102 Additionally, it is found to be effective in the 
treatment of knee arthritis. 99 Strontium citrate is available in the 
United States as a nutritional supplement. Animal studies indi -
cated that strontium citrate is taken up by bone in greater concen-
tration than the ranelate form, 127 although no clinical trials have 
yet been conducted with the citrate form. The higher atomic 
number of strontium (38, compared with 20 for calcium) may 
create difficulty in the assessment of treatment efficacy based on 
dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry scans.
74
Essential Oils
Essential oils are concentrated from plants. They are mostly used 
aromatically or topically to improve physical, mental, or spiritual 
well-being. There are many uses for essential oils that include 
stress and anxiety reduction, pain management, and improvement 
of nausea and vomiting. However, it is important to know that 
these products are not monitored by the US Food and Drug 
 Administration. So, although most sources state that there are 
none to limited adverse effects of essential oils, there is no true 
regulation over the substances in each container.
Economics of Complementary and 
Integrative Health Care
It is well documented that rising costs are a tremendous challenge 
to health care in the United States. The Institute of Medicine 
found, in 2008, that the cost for treating pain in the United States 
alone was between $560 and $635 billion.
54 This number in-
cludes the cost of health care plus lost productivity. With this 
rising cost and the concern about health outcomes, systems are 
moving toward redesigning how health care is provided. In 2011, 
the Veterans Health Administration (VHA) created the Office of 
Patient Centered Care and Cultural T ransformation with the 
goals of moving the VHA’s focus on episodic care to continuous 
engagement throughout the lifetime of the individual and increas-
ing access to integrative approaches. In 2020, the Department of 
Veterans Affairs (VA) published a compendium on use of CIH. In 
this document, the VA demonstrates an increase in use of CIH by 
over 70% between fiscal years 2017 and 2019.
115
In 2009, Abbott Northwestern Hospital in Minneapolis, 
Minnesota, undertook a retrospective study to examine non -
toxic, nonpharmacologic integrative approaches to its inpatient 
population. A total of 1839 inpatients, two-thirds of whom had 
never had CIH, underwent various CIH practices that included 
acupuncture, massage, reflexology (foot massage), music ther -
apy, guided imagery, relaxation response stress reduction, and

Using CIH within a medical system may lead to economic cost

such, it is important for future research to examine this area, es -

Summary
Integrative health care is accurately described as an approach to 
the delivery of health care, not as a separate entity in and of itself. 
In fact, many believe that integrative health care is the way all 
medical care, not just rehabilitation, will be in the future. 
 Although it often incorporates many complementary practices, 
the key is a patient-centered integrative approach that includes all 
health and wellness practices that best serve each individual.
